pages.title: Roles and Responsibilities of Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Roles and Responsibilities of Security Professionals

### Definition of a Security Guard

According to the **Security Services and Investigators Act (SSIA)**, a security guard is defined as an individual who will:

> "Protect an organization's property, personnel and information against fire, theft, vandalism and illegal entry."

Throughout this course, the broader term **"security professional"** is used to acknowledge the wide variety of duties and responsibilities associated with security work.

### Related Job Titles

Security professionals may work under various titles, including:

- Security guard
- Loss prevention officer
- Executive security
- Private investigator
- In-house investigator
- Locksmith
- Patrol dog handler
- Alarm responder

Regardless of job title, all security professionals perform similar tasks and are governed by the same Act to ensure minimum standards of training, accountability, and professionalism.

### Primary Role

The primary role of a security professional can be summarized in three words:

**OBSERVE – DETER – REPORT**

This core principle guides all security work and should be at the forefront of your daily activities.

### Common Duties and Activities

In the course of your duties as a security professional, you will be expected to perform activities such as:

- **Patrolling** of premises and/or grounds
- **Monitoring alarm systems** and responding when appropriate
- **Limiting access** to individuals or locations
- **Observing and reporting** criminal activity or signs of a crime
- **Directing traffic**
- **Responding to emergencies**
- **Documenting** interactions and events
- **Presenting a professional image**
- **Arresting** those found committing a crime (when legally permitted)

### The Three Areas of Protection

Security professionals are responsible for protecting three key areas:

#### 1. Protection of Persons

Being responsible for the safety and well-being of individuals may involve:

- Physically protecting a person (such as serving as a bodyguard)
- Looking out for hazards when groups of people are present (for example, at large concerts or events)
- Ensuring public safety in various environments

#### 2. Protection of Property

When protecting property, you are primarily concerned with:

**Human threats:**
- Vandalism
- Theft
- Disturbances and fights
- Criminal acts
- Unauthorized entry

**Natural threats:**
- Flooding
- Fire
- Weather-related damage
- Environmental hazards

#### 3. Protection of Information

Protecting information involves:

- **Physical protection:** Guarding locations where information (such as computer data) is stored
- **Confidentiality:** Withholding confidential information you have been entrusted with, such as:
  - The whereabouts of a person
  - Location of items
  - Details about events
  - Business operations

### Information Security in the Digital Age

As computers play an increasingly important role in business and daily life, there are growing efforts to protect data stored on hard drives and servers. While you might be specifically assigned to protect workers or a business location, you are also in a position to notice when:

- People are accessing computers in an unusual manner
- Computer access is occurring at suspicious times
- Unauthorized individuals are attempting to access information storage devices

If you notice an individual or group accessing a computer in a manner or at a time that seems out of place, unauthorized access to information may be occurring. **Your responsibility is to observe and report** such activity according to your organization's procedures.

### Key Takeaway

As a security professional, you serve a vital role in protecting people, property, and information. Your effectiveness depends on your ability to remain observant, deter potential threats through your presence and professionalism, and report accurately on incidents and concerns.

pages.title: Security Licensing Requirements in Alberta
pages.body:
## Security Licensing Requirements in Alberta

### The Security Services and Investigators Act (SSIA)

Alberta introduced the **Security Services and Investigators Act (SSIA)** on **June 1, 2010** to:

- Reflect changes in the security industry in recent years
- Provide an industry-wide standard across the province
- Establish legal requirements for individuals and businesses working in security

The Act is accompanied by two key documents:
- **Security Services and Investigators Regulation**
- **Security Services and Investigators (Ministerial) Regulation**

These regulations prescribe how the law will be administered and enforced.

### Who Needs a Licence?

According to **Section 3 of the SSIA**, no person may, without a licence and for remuneration (payment):

- Patrol, guard, or provide security for another person or their property/premises
- Detect loss of or damage to another person's property or premises
- Advertise or offer to provide these services

**Loss prevention workers** (Section 6) also require a licence to:

- Work in plain clothes for payment
- Prevent or detect loss/damage to commercial, industrial, or retail property

**Executive security** professionals guard or provide protection to a specific individual requiring personal protection. This licence class is exempt from wearing a uniform if desired, though any uniform worn must be compliant with legislation.

### Types of Security Licences in Alberta

The Alberta Basic Security Training (ABST) course applies to the following classes of licence:

- **Security Services**
- **Loss Prevention**
- **Executive Protection**
- **Patrol Dog Handler**
- **Alarm Responder**

Additional approved training is required for **baton** carrying permissions.

### Individual Licence Requirements

To apply for a security professional licence in Alberta, you must meet ALL of the following requirements:

#### Age and Legal Status
- Be **18 years of age** at the time of application
- Be a Canadian citizen OR legally entitled to work in Canada

#### Character and Criminal Record
- Be **competent and of good character**
- Have **no serious criminal record** for which no pardon has been received
- Have **no outstanding criminal charges**
- Must not be the subject of an ongoing criminal investigation

#### Language Skills
- Must be **fluent in English***
- Able to communicate effectively with emergency services personnel and the public

*Note: Where an applicant's English language skills are in question, language proficiency testing may be required.*

#### Training
- Successful completion of an **approved training course** for the class of licence being sought (such as this ABST course)
- Successful completion of approved baton training (if seeking permission to carry a baton)

### Application Process

To become licensed, follow these steps:

1. **Complete** the application form for individual licence
2. **Provide copies** of approved identification with application form
3. **Provide proof** of approved training with application form
4. **Provide a Police Information and Criminal Record Check** document, including:
   - CPIC (Canadian Police Information Centre) search
   - Vulnerable Sector search
   - Local Database searches
5. **Provide one photograph** signed by local police service
6. **Mail or courier** the complete application package with the appropriate fee to the Security Programs office

Visit **www.securityprograms.alberta.ca** for application forms and detailed information.

### Important Restrictions

**You are NOT permitted to work until you have received your licence.**

Your employer will require a copy of both the front and back of your licence when you begin employment.

**Your licence is NOT transferable** – it cannot be given or transferred to another person or used at another company without proper process.

### Grounds for Licence Refusal

According to **Section 16 of the SSIA**, the Registrar may refuse to issue or renew a licence if the applicant:

- Has contravened or is contravening the Act or regulations
- Has not met the requirements of the Act or regulations
- Has provided false or misleading information
- Has not complied with licence terms or conditions (for renewals)
- Is not, in the opinion of the Registrar, a fit and proper person to hold a licence
- **Has been charged with a criminal offence**
- Is not in the public interest to be licensed

### Licensee Obligations and Reporting Requirements

Once licensed, you have ongoing obligations under **Section 18 of the Act** and **Section 3 of the Regulation**:

#### Report to the Registrar in writing within specified timeframes if:

**Within 24 hours:**
- You are **arrested or charged** with an offence under the Criminal Code, Controlled Drugs and Substances Act, or any other Canadian enactment
- You **lose your licence** (the physical card)

**Within 30 days:**
- You **change your address**
- There is a **change in any information** you provided when you applied (e.g., updated criminal record information, renewed work permit)

#### Consequences of Non-Compliance

If you fail to comply with reporting requirements, the Registrar may:
- Cancel your licence
- Suspend your licence
- Impose additional terms and conditions on your licence

### Licence Suspension Conditions

According to **Section 20 of the SSIA**, an individual licence may be suspended under various conditions, including but not limited to failure to meet ongoing requirements and violations of the Act or regulations.

### Prohibited Titles

An individual holding a security professional licence **may NOT refer to themselves as:**

- Private detective
- Law enforcement officer
- Protection officer
- Security officer

Using these prohibited titles can result in penalties under the Act.

### Key Takeaway

Obtaining and maintaining a security licence in Alberta requires meeting specific legal requirements, completing approved training, maintaining good character, and fulfilling ongoing reporting obligations. Understanding and complying with these requirements is essential for a successful career in the security industry.

pages.title: Public Complaints Process for Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Public Complaints Process for Security Professionals

### Overview

The **Security Services and Investigators Act (SSIA)** establishes a formal process for members of the public to file complaints against security professionals. This process ensures accountability and professionalism in the security industry while providing fair treatment to both complainants and security professionals.

### Time Limit for Filing Complaints

A complaint against an individual holding a security licence must be made **within 90 days** of the occurrence of the alleged incident.

### Step 1: Submission of Complaint

#### Where to Submit

The complainant must submit a **written complaint** to the **owner of the business or organization** employing the security professional who is the subject of the complaint.

#### Required Contents

A complaint must contain:

- **The reason for the complaint**
- **Details about the incident** for which the complaint is being made

#### Important Note

The complaint must be:
- In writing
- Filed within **90 days** of the alleged incident
- Directed to the employer/business owner (not directly to government)

### Step 2: Informal Resolution and Mediation (Optional)

**With the consent of both parties**, the employer may attempt to resolve the issue between:

- The security professional, and
- The complainant

This informal resolution process:
- Is voluntary and requires agreement from both parties
- Can provide a faster resolution
- May resolve the matter without formal investigation

### Step 3: Investigation of Complaint

If informal resolution is not pursued or is unsuccessful, a formal investigation takes place:

#### Within 30 Days of Receipt

The organization will provide **written acknowledgement** of the complaint to:
- The complainant
- The licensed security professional who is the subject of the complaint

#### Within 90 Days of Receipt

The organization will:
- Complete the investigation into the complaint
- Provide **written notice of the outcome** to both:
  - The complainant
  - The licensed security professional

#### Special Circumstance: Criminal Activity

**If criminal activity is alleged**, a **police investigation** will take place. This runs parallel to or may supersede the administrative complaint process.

### Step 4: Review by the Registrar (Appeal Level 1)

If the complainant is not satisfied with the decision made by the organization/business owner, they may request a review.

#### Process:

- The complainant must make a **written request** to the office of the **Registrar**
- This request must be submitted **within 30 days** of receiving written notice of the employer/organization's decision
- The Registrar will review the matter and make a decision

### Step 5: Review by the Director of Law Enforcement (Appeal Level 2 - Final)

If the complainant remains unsatisfied after the Registrar's review, a final level of appeal is available.

#### Process:

- The complainant must submit a **written request** for review to the **Director of Law Enforcement** at the office of the Solicitor General and Ministry of Public Security
- The Director will advise the complainant, **in writing**, of:
  - The review process
  - The timeline associated with the review
- **The decision of the Director is FINAL** – no further appeals are available under this process

### Rights of the Security Professional

As a licensed security professional, you have specific rights throughout the complaints process:

#### Right to Notification

- You must be informed in writing when a complaint is filed against you (within 30 days)
- You must receive written notice of the outcome of the investigation (within 90 days)

#### Right to Participate

- You may participate in informal resolution/mediation (with your consent)
- You have the opportunity to provide your account of events during the investigation

#### Right to Fair Treatment

- The investigation must be conducted fairly and in accordance with the Act
- Decisions must be based on evidence and facts

### What Complaints May Involve

Complaints against security professionals may relate to:

- **Conduct issues:** Unprofessional behavior, rudeness, inappropriate language
- **Use of force:** Allegations of excessive or unnecessary force
- **Discrimination:** Unfair treatment based on protected grounds
- **Abuse of authority:** Overstepping legal boundaries or misuse of position
- **Neglect of duty:** Failure to perform required duties
- **Violations of the Code of Conduct:** Breach of professional standards
- **Criminal allegations:** Theft, assault, or other criminal behavior

### Best Practices to Avoid Complaints

As a security professional, you can minimize the risk of complaints by:

- **Following the law** and your employer's policies at all times
- **Documenting your actions** thoroughly and accurately
- **Communicating professionally** and respectfully with all individuals
- **Using only necessary force** when required, and documenting its use
- **Acting with integrity and honesty** in all situations
- **Knowing your authority** and staying within legal boundaries
- **Maintaining professional appearance and conduct**
- **Following the Code of Conduct** required by the Ministerial Regulation

### Consequences of Substantiated Complaints

If a complaint is found to be valid, consequences may include:

- Written warning
- Additional training requirements
- Suspension of licence
- Cancellation of licence
- Additional terms and conditions placed on your licence
- Criminal charges (if criminal activity is substantiated)

### Key Takeaway

The public complaints process under the SSIA ensures accountability in the security profession. Understanding this process helps you recognize the importance of professional conduct and the serious consequences of violations. By maintaining high professional standards, you protect both the public you serve and your own career in the security industry.

**Remember:** The public has the right to file a complaint without informing you directly that they are doing so. The complaint goes to your employer, who then notifies you as part of the formal process.

pages.title: Professional Appearance and Uniform Requirements
pages.body:
## Professional Appearance and Uniform Requirements

### The Importance of First Impressions

**"You never get a second chance to make a first impression."**

This common expression is particularly true for security professionals. The public will immediately recognize and respect – or not respect – you in your role based on:

- Your appearance
- How you conduct yourself

If your appearance is unkempt and your manner casual or inappropriate, people will likely think you hold equal disregard for the job you are tasked to do.

### The Professional Advantage

**Putting on a professional attitude** and taking care with your appearance provides multiple benefits:

- **Helps you feel better about yourself**
- **Inspires confidence** in the people who are looking to you for guidance and protection
- **Commands respect** from the public
- **Enhances your effectiveness** in your role

### The Bottom Line

**IF YOU TAKE YOURSELF SERIOUSLY, OTHERS WILL TAKE YOU SERIOUSLY.**

### Legal Requirements: Uniforms and Weapons

Your apparel while working as a security professional is governed by legislation.

**Section 34 of the Security Services and Investigators Act (SSIA) states:**

#### Section 34(1) – Uniforms
"An individual licensee must wear the uniform and insignia specified in the regulations for that class of licensees."

#### Section 34(2) – Weapons and Equipment
"An individual licensee shall not have in the licensee's possession any weapons or equipment except those specified in the regulations or authorized by the Registrar."

### Employer Dress Code

Your employer will designate your work attire in accordance with the SSIA provisions. Your required uniform may vary depending on:

- The type of assignment you are given
- The work environment
- Client requirements
- Safety considerations

#### Examples of Uniform Variations:

**Standard Security Uniform:**
- Traditional security guard uniform with company insignia

**Safety-Oriented Clothing:**
- High-visibility vest or jacket
- Hard hat or protective headgear
- Safety boots
- Required for large events or traffic direction

**Business Attire:**
- Professional business clothing
- Required for executive protection or loss prevention work
- May be "plain clothes" for loss prevention

**Specialized Equipment:**
- Weather-appropriate outerwear
- Protective equipment for specific environments

### Professional Appearance Standards

Even if your company does not provide specific guidelines beyond the uniform, it is in your best interest to maintain a professional appearance:

#### Uniform Care

- **Clean:** Uniform should be washed regularly and free of stains
- **Good repair:** No rips, tears, or missing buttons
- **Pressed:** Iron or steam to remove wrinkles
- **Properly fitted:** Uniform should fit appropriately, not too tight or too loose

#### Personal Grooming

**Hair:**
- Clean and neatly combed
- Long hair should be securely tied back for:
  - **Neatness**
  - **Safety purposes** (prevents grabbing in physical confrontations)

**Facial Hair:**
- Well-groomed and trimmed
- Should not appear unkempt

**Hygiene:**
- Daily shower/bathing
- Use of deodorant
- Clean hands and nails
- Fresh breath (especially important for public interaction)

#### Footwear

- **Clean and shined**
- **Comfortable** – you may be on your feet for long periods
- **Appropriate for the environment** (slip-resistant, safety-rated if required)
- **Professional appearance**

**Note:** Comfortable shoes will help you maintain a professional demeanor and prevent an unprofessional frown on your face.

#### Jewellery

- Should be **simple and not excessive**
- Avoid:
  - Long necklaces (can be grabbed)
  - Dangling earrings (can be pulled)
  - Multiple rings (may cause injury to you or others)
  - Facial piercings that could be caught or torn

**Safety consideration:** Jewellery can be a hazard in situations where you must protect yourself or others.

#### Tattoos and Piercings

- You may be asked to **cover up piercings or tattoos**
- Some clients may feel visible tattoos or piercings are unprofessional
- Understand and comply with your employer's policy
- Use cover-up makeup, bandages, or long sleeves if required

### Personal Health and Wellness

Maintaining good health is essential for professional appearance and job performance:

#### Adequate Sleep
- Get sufficient rest between shifts
- Particularly important for night shift workers
- Fatigue shows in your face and affects your alertness

#### Proper Nutrition
- Eat balanced, healthy meals
- Stay hydrated throughout your shift
- Avoid excessive caffeine or energy drinks
- Pack healthy snacks for long shifts

#### Regular Exercise
- Maintains physical fitness for job demands
- Helps manage stress
- Improves overall health and appearance
- Provides strength needed for physical aspects of the job

#### Benefits of Good Health:
- **Best defence against fatigue** during late-night or long shifts
- **Provides physical strength** in case you need to defend yourself or clients
- **Improves mental alertness** and decision-making
- **Enhances professional appearance** (posture, energy, demeanor)

### Key Takeaway

Your professional appearance is a critical component of your effectiveness as a security professional. Taking care of your mind and body, maintaining a clean and proper uniform, and presenting yourself professionally will:

- Earn respect from the public
- Inspire confidence in your abilities
- Make you feel better about your role
- Enhance your safety and job performance
- Reflect positively on your employer and the security profession

**Remember:** You are often the first point of contact for people seeking help or information. Your appearance sets the tone for all interactions and demonstrates your commitment to your profession.

pages.title: Professional Conduct and Deportment for Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Professional Conduct and Deportment for Security Professionals

### Understanding Deportment and Demeanor

**Deportment** describes the conduct and behavior of an individual. It has traditionally been associated with the military, law enforcement, and the security industry.

**Demeanor** describes how an individual responds to:
- Other people
- Their environment
- Challenging situations

Good deportment is necessary in your interactions with:
- The public
- Your colleagues
- Your supervisor
- Other members of your organization

### The Team Concept

**Remember: You are part of a team.**

Helping your team be the best they can will ultimately:
- Make you successful
- Keep you safe on the job
- Improve overall security effectiveness
- Build a positive reputation for your organization

### Code of Conduct (Ministerial Regulation Section 20)

To ensure program integrity, all security professionals are held to a common standard through a **Code of Conduct** required by Section 20 of the Ministerial Regulation.

#### Purpose:
- Ensures minimum standards across the industry
- Strengthens program integrity
- Provides consistency in service delivery
- Establishes clear behavioral expectations

#### Responsibility:
**Business licensees** are directly responsible for:
- Ensuring internal human resource documentation incorporates the required Code of Conduct
- May add additional standards to meet agency or corporate needs
- The Code of Conduct in the regulation represents **minimum standards** and is not exhaustive

### Code of Conduct Requirements

**While on duty**, every licensee (business or individual) shall abide by the following:

## What a Licensee WILL Do:

### 1. Act with Honesty and Integrity
- Be truthful in all communications
- Maintain ethical standards
- Demonstrate trustworthiness
- Honor commitments and responsibilities

### 2. Comply with All Laws
- **Federal laws** (e.g., Criminal Code, Controlled Drugs and Substances Act)
- **Provincial laws** (e.g., SSIA, Occupational Health and Safety)
- **Municipal laws** (e.g., bylaws, local ordinances)

### 3. Respect Privacy
- Treat all information received while working as **confidential**
- **Exceptions** where disclosure is permitted:
  - Required as part of security work
  - Required by law
  - Required under the Personal Information Protection Act

### 4. Abide by Employer's Code of Conduct
- Follow your employer's specific code of conduct
- Employer standards are **in addition to** the legislated Code of Conduct
- Understand and comply with company policies and procedures

## What a Licensee WILL NOT Do:

### 1. Engage in Disorderly or Inappropriate Conduct

**Examples include:**
- Fighting (unless in lawful self-defense)
- Using profane or abusive language
- Harassment of any kind
- Intimidation or bullying
- Sexual misconduct
- Disrespectful behavior toward others

### 2. Use Unnecessary Force

- Force must be **reasonable and necessary**
- Only use force when legally justified
- Use the **minimum force required** to accomplish a lawful objective
- Document any use of force thoroughly

### 3. Withhold or Suppress Information

**Do not hide or fail to report:**
- Complaints about any other licensee
- Reports about any other licensee's misconduct
- Information relevant to investigations
- Safety concerns or violations

**This requirement promotes:**
- Accountability within the profession
- Public safety
- Professional integrity

### 4. Make False, Misleading, or Inaccurate Statements

**Do not:**
- Willfully make false statements (intentionally lying)
- Negligently make false statements (careless with facts)
- Sign false documentation
- Provide misleading information
- Create inaccurate reports

**Applies to:**
- Written reports
- Verbal statements
- Official documentation
- Testimony or statements to authorities

### 5. Consume Alcohol

- **Zero tolerance** while on duty
- Impairs judgment and reaction time
- Creates safety risks
- Violates professional standards
- May result in immediate termination and licence suspension/cancellation

### 6. Consume Controlled Drugs or Substances

**Prohibited under the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act (Canada):**
- Illegal drugs (marijuana, cocaine, methamphetamine, etc.)
- Prescription drugs not prescribed to you
- Misuse of prescribed medications

**Consequences:**
- Impairs your ability to perform duties safely
- Illegal activity
- Immediate grounds for termination
- Criminal charges
- Licence cancellation

### 7. Possess Prohibited Drugs or Substances

- Cannot possess controlled drugs/substances where possession is prohibited by the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act (Canada)
- Applies both on and off duty (criminal matter)
- Possession must be reported to Registrar within 24 hours if charged

### Practical Applications of Good Conduct

#### Communication
- Speak clearly and professionally
- Listen actively to others
- Remain calm and courteous under pressure
- Use appropriate language (no profanity)

#### Interaction with the Public
- Be helpful and approachable
- Show respect to all individuals
- Remain impartial and unbiased
- Maintain professional boundaries

#### Interaction with Colleagues
- Support your team members
- Communicate effectively
- Share relevant information
- Offer assistance when appropriate
- Resolve conflicts professionally

#### Handling Difficult Situations
- Remain calm and composed
- Use de-escalation techniques
- Follow established procedures
- Request backup when needed
- Document incidents thoroughly

#### Maintaining Confidentiality
- Do not discuss sensitive information in public
- Protect client and company information
- Only share information on a "need to know" basis
- Secure documents and electronic data
- Do not post about work on social media

### Consequences of Code Violations

Violating the Code of Conduct may result in:

**From Employer:**
- Verbal or written warning
- Suspension from duties
- Termination of employment

**From Registrar:**
- Additional terms and conditions on licence
- Suspension of licence
- Cancellation of licence
- Fines or penalties

**Legal Consequences:**
- Criminal charges (for illegal activities)
- Civil liability (for negligence or misconduct)
- Loss of ability to work in security industry

### Best Practices for Professional Conduct

#### DO:
- Treat everyone with respect and dignity
- Follow all policies, procedures, and laws
- Report incidents and concerns promptly
- Maintain confidentiality
- Present a professional image at all times
- Continue learning and improving your skills
- Take responsibility for your actions

#### DON'T:
- Use your position for personal gain
- Engage in discriminatory behavior
- Ignore safety concerns or violations
- Make assumptions without facts
- Discuss sensitive information inappropriately
- Let personal feelings affect your professionalism

### Key Takeaway

Professional conduct and deportment are fundamental to success as a security professional. The Code of Conduct provides clear standards for behavior, ensuring that all security professionals maintain the integrity and professionalism required by the industry. By following these standards, you protect yourself, your colleagues, the public, and the reputation of the security profession.

**Your conduct reflects not only on you personally, but on your employer, the security industry, and the profession as a whole.**

pages.title: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for Security Professionals

### Overview

The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms applies to **everyone** in Canada, including security professionals. When you work in security, you must respect and protect the same rights you have as an individual. Understanding these rights is not a limitation on your job—it is a protection that helps you avoid legal action and civil suits.

### Key Sections for Security Professionals

Sections 7 through 14 of the Charter are particularly important for security work. The most critical are:

#### Section 7: Life, Liberty and Security of Person
- Everyone has the right to life, freedom, and safety
- These rights cannot be taken away except according to fundamental justice
- **In simpler terms:** You cannot take away someone's freedom without proper legal grounds

#### Section 8: Search or Seizure
- Everyone has the right to be secure against unreasonable search or seizure
- **In simpler terms:** You cannot search people or take their property without proper authority or consent

#### Section 9: Detention or Imprisonment
- Everyone has the right not to be arbitrarily detained or imprisoned
- **In simpler terms:** You cannot hold someone against their will without adequate reason

#### Section 10: Arrest or Detention Rights
When a person is arrested or detained, they have the right to:
- Be informed promptly of the reasons
- Retain and instruct a lawyer without delay
- Be informed of their right to a lawyer
- Have the validity of detention determined and be released if unlawful

### Important Principle: You Are NOT a Police Officer

**Critical fact:** As a security professional, you have the **same rights as any other citizen**—no more, no fewer. You should never confuse your position with the powers granted to police officers or peace officers.

### Your Primary Duty

Your main job as a security professional is to:
- **Observe and report** criminal activity
- Safeguard people and property
- Leave law enforcement to the police

### Protection Through Knowledge

By knowing what is permissible and acting accordingly, you:
- Limit your vulnerability to legal action
- Protect your professional reputation
- Protect your employer's reputation
- Save time and money for all parties involved

### Rights of Arrested Persons

Remember: An individual who has been arrested **still has rights**. You must be respectful of those rights. Our justice system requires:
- Criminal suspects be given a fair trial
- Presumption of innocence until convicted
- Respectful treatment at all times

pages.title: Citizen's Power of Arrest Under Section 494
pages.body:
## Citizen's Power of Arrest Under Section 494

### What is Arrest?

**Arrest** is the process of detaining an individual for the purposes of investigating their possible involvement in a criminal activity. To arrest someone means to **take away their freedom**.

### Legal Authority: Criminal Code Section 494

Under the Criminal Code of Canada, individuals who are NOT peace officers (including security professionals) may arrest persons in specific situations.

### Section 494(1)(a): Finding Someone Committing an Indictable Offence

**You may arrest without a warrant:**
- A person whom you **find committing** an indictable offence

**Important details:**
- "Find committing" means you must **catch them in the act**
- It is NOT enough to see the start or the end result—you must witness the act itself
- This applies ONLY to **indictable offences** or **hybrid offences**, NOT summary conviction offences
- Examples: assault, theft over $5,000, break and enter, robbery

**Example:** You witness an individual breaking a window and climbing into a building. This is break and enter (indictable offence) and you are witnessing it happen. You may arrest at that moment.

### Section 494(1)(b): Assisting in Pursuit

**You may arrest without a warrant:**
- A person who is escaping from and being freshly pursued by someone with lawful authority to arrest

**Requirements:**
- You must have **reasonable grounds** to believe a criminal offence has been committed
- The person must be actively running away
- Someone with lawful authority (police officer, building employee, loss prevention worker) must be pursuing them

**Example:** You hear a burglar alarm and see a police officer chasing someone while yelling "STOP, you are under arrest!" You may assist in stopping and arresting that person.

### Section 494(2): Property Owner or Agent Authority

**As a security professional acting on behalf of a property owner, you may arrest:**
- Any person found committing a **criminal offence** on or in relation to that property

**Key points:**
- This applies to ANY criminal offence (not just indictable)
- Lower threshold than section 494(1)(a)
- You can arrest at the time OR within a reasonable time after the offence
- Only if it is not feasible for a police officer to make the arrest

**Example 1:** You work loss prevention in a retail store. You witness a person take goods and exit without paying. You may arrest on behalf of the store owner.

**Example 2:** You guard an office building. You witness someone steal a laptop in the food court. This is a criminal offence on the property. You may arrest.

### Section 494(2)(b): Arrest Within Reasonable Time

**New provision (amended 2012):**
- You may arrest within a "reasonable amount of time" after witnessing an offence
- Only if a peace officer is not available to make the arrest

**Example:** On Monday, you witnessed a customer steal a $6,000 ring but they escaped. On Wednesday, they return to the store. You MAY arrest under this section because:
- You personally witnessed the original offence
- You are making the arrest within reasonable time
- **Important:** Always call police and have them make the arrest if possible

### Section 494(3): Delivery to Police

**Mandatory requirement:**
- When you arrest someone under Section 494, you MUST turn them over to a peace officer **forthwith** (immediately)
- Call police to come to your location
- Be prepared to provide a statement

### When You CANNOT Arrest

**You may NOT make a citizen's arrest when:**
- You did not personally witness the offence
- Someone else tells you they think they saw an offence
- You only see the aftermath of a crime
- The offence is a summary conviction offence (unless you are acting as property owner/agent)

**Example:** A patron tells you they think they saw someone shoplift. You did NOT witness it yourself. You cannot arrest.

### Best Practices

1. **Discuss with your employer** what their policy is on arrest—some employers prohibit staff from making arrests
2. **Wherever possible, do not arrest yourself**—call police and be a good witness
3. **Be observant** and take clear, detailed notes
4. **Know the difference** between summary conviction, hybrid, and indictable offences
5. **Understand reasonable grounds** before taking action

### Reasonable Grounds Defined

**Reasonable grounds** describe a situation where the facts and information available would lead an average person to conclude a criminal act has occurred.

It is based on:
- What you can see
- What you can hear
- The circumstances of the situation
- Objective facts, not just suspicion

pages.title: Types of Criminal Offences in the Criminal Code
pages.body:
## Types of Criminal Offences in the Criminal Code

### Overview

The Criminal Code of Canada defines three classifications of offences. Understanding these categories is critical because your power to arrest depends on the type of offence committed.

### Three Classifications of Offences

#### 1. Summary Conviction Offences

**Description:**
- Considered "less serious offences"
- Punishable by jail time, fine, or both
- **You CANNOT arrest for these offences under Section 494(1)(a)**
- You CAN arrest if acting as property owner/agent under Section 494(2)

**Common Examples:**
- Causing a disturbance
- Unlawful assembly
- Indecent acts
- Loitering
- Trespassing at night
- Vagrancy
- Weapon at public meeting

#### 2. Indictable Offences

**Description:**
- Serious offences defined in federal law
- Punishments are more serious than summary conviction
- **You CAN arrest for these under Section 494(1)(a) if you witness the act**

**Common Examples:**
- Weapon for dangerous purpose
- Aggravated assault
- Theft over $5,000
- Robbery with firearm
- Robbery, other
- Break and enter with intent (dwelling house)
- Possession of property over $5,000
- Fraud over $5,000
- Arson (disregard for human life)
- Arson (damage to property of others)
- Mischief endangering life
- Possession of counterfeit money

#### 3. Hybrid (Dual) Offences

**Description:**
- Offences that include both a summary conviction component AND an indictable component
- The Crown prosecutor decides which way to proceed
- The individual will be prosecuted for only ONE type
- **You CAN arrest for these under Section 494(1)(a) if you witness the act**

**Common Examples:**
- Possession of prohibited weapon
- Public mischief
- Failure to stop at accident scene
- Criminal harassment
- Assault (common assault)
- Assault causing bodily harm
- Assault with a weapon
- Sexual assault
- Take motor vehicle without consent
- Theft $5,000 or less
- Obtain food/lodging by fraud
- Obtain transportation by fraud
- Possession of housebreaking instruments
- Break and enter (non-dwelling house)
- Possession of property under $5,000
- Forgery
- Utter forged document
- Fraud $5,000 or less
- Mischief to property over $5,000
- Mischief, other property
- Mischief, data
- False alarm of fire

### Six Most Common Offences for Security Professionals

As a security professional, you are most likely to encounter:

1. **Defence of Property**
2. **Assault**
3. **Theft**
4. **Break and Enter**
5. **Possession of Stolen Property**
6. **Mischief**

### 1. Defence of Property (Section 35)

**As a security worker authorized by the property owner, you may arrest if:**
- You believe on reasonable grounds that someone is about to:
  - Enter without permission
  - Take property
  - Damage or destroy property
  - Make property inoperative

### 2. Assault (Section 265)

**Assault occurs when:**
- One person uses force on another without consent
- One person threatens another or causes them to believe they will carry out a threat

**Examples of force:**
- Hitting, punching
- Kicking
- Spitting
- Pushing/shoving
- Grabbing
- Threatening gestures

### 3. Theft (Section 322)

**Theft is committed when someone:**
- Fraudulently and without colour of right takes something
- Converts something to their use or another person's use
- With intent to deprive the owner (temporarily or absolutely)

**Theft is complete when:**
- The person moves the item with intent to steal

**Retail Theft Best Practice:**
- Do NOT consider theft complete until the subject has passed checkout/till area AND left the store
- This prevents the defence: "I was looking for other items and intended to pay"

### 4. Break and Enter (Section 348)

**Break and enter occurs when someone:**
- Breaks into a premises with intent to commit an indictable offence

**"Breaking" means:**
- Physically damaging a premises to enter (e.g., smashing window)
- Opening something that covers an opening (e.g., picking a lock)

### 5. Possession of Stolen Property (Section 354)

**An offence is committed when someone:**
- Has in their possession any property or proceeds
- Knowing it was obtained by or derived from a crime
- Either in Canada or anywhere else

### 6. Mischief (Section 430)

**Mischief occurs when someone wilfully:**
- Destroys or damages property
- Renders property dangerous, useless, inoperative, or ineffective
- Obstructs or interferes with lawful use or enjoyment of property
- Obstructs or interferes with any person's lawful use of property

**Mischief in relation to data:**
- Destroys or alters data
- Renders data meaningless or useless
- Interferes with lawful use of data
- Denies access to data

**Examples:**
- Vandalism
- Tampering with computer data
- Breaking into a parked vehicle

pages.title: Legal Use of Force by Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Legal Use of Force by Security Professionals

### The Most Important Rule

**Avoid the use of force as much as possible.**

Most situations can be handled through skilled communication. Escalating an incident to one requiring force is risky for:
- Your physical well-being
- Your professional reputation
- Your legal standing

### Fundamental Principle

**As a security professional, you have no more and no fewer rights than any other citizen.**

Your authority to use force is exactly the same as any private individual in Canada.

### Legal Authority: Section 25 of the Criminal Code

Section 25 outlines who is permitted to use force and under what circumstances.

#### Section 25(1): When Force is Justified

**You may use force when:**
- You are required or authorized by law to do something
- You act on **reasonable grounds**
- You use only **as much force as is necessary** for that purpose

**This applies to:**
- Private persons (including security professionals)
- Peace officers
- Public officers
- Anyone aiding a peace or public officer

### When You May Use Reasonable Force

**1. Aiding a Police or Peace Officer**
- To stop the flight of a suspected criminal
- To settle a disturbance
- At the officer's request

**2. Self-Defence or Defence of Others**
- Protecting yourself from harm or injury
- Protecting others from harm or injury

**3. Stopping a Criminal Act**
- Preventing someone from committing a criminal offence
- Example: Pulling someone back who is attempting to break into a home

### Section 25(3): Limitation on Deadly Force

**You are NOT justified in using force that is intended or likely to cause death or grievous bodily harm UNLESS:**
- You believe on reasonable grounds it is necessary for:
  - Self-preservation, OR
  - Preservation of anyone under your protection
- From death or grievous bodily harm

### Section 26: Criminal Responsibility for Excessive Force

**Critical Warning:**

**You can be charged with a criminal offence if you use excessive force.**

Even if not criminally charged, you may:
- Face discipline or dismissal by your employer
- Be charged in a civil lawsuit
- Damage your professional reputation

### What is Excessive Force?

"Reasonable force" is determined by the situation. The amount of force must be:
- Consistent with the physical and mental characteristics of the subject
- Appropriate to the situation at hand

#### Example 1: Small Female Shoplifter

**Situation:**
- Small female suspect
- Stole cosmetic items
- Appears afraid
- Begins to run when approached

**Appropriate Response:**
- Grab by wrist or elbow to stop flight
- Subject appears fearful, not violent
- Small stature won't require excessive struggle
- Public setting requires minimal force display

**Why this is appropriate:**
- Minimal force needed based on subject's characteristics
- Avoids drawing unwanted attention
- Prevents escalation

#### Example 2: Intoxicated Fighters at Stadium

**Situation:**
- Two intoxicated individuals fighting
- Large public venue
- Police officer directs you to assist

**Appropriate Response:**
- Apply enough force to pull individuals apart
- Restrain one person pending further instructions
- Maintain control without hitting or kicking

**Why this is appropriate:**
- Subjects are emotionally heated and intoxicated
- They may continue to resist
- Restraining is sufficient—no need for strikes
- Maintains clear distinction between security and fighters
- Helps police, doesn't add to their workload

### Section 27: Use of Force to Prevent Commission of Offence

**You are justified in using as much force as is reasonably necessary to prevent:**

**An offence that:**
- Could result in arrest without warrant, AND
- Would likely cause immediate and serious injury to any person or property

**OR anything you believe on reasonable grounds would be such an offence**

### Key Factors in Determining Reasonable Force

1. **Physical characteristics of the subject**
   - Size, strength, age

2. **Mental state of the subject**
   - Intoxicated, agitated, fearful, calm

3. **Nature of the offence**
   - Violent vs. non-violent
   - Serious vs. minor

4. **Environment**
   - Public vs. private
   - Presence of bystanders
   - Available backup

5. **Level of resistance**
   - Passive vs. active resistance
   - Aggressive vs. defensive behaviour

### Professional Recommendations

**Use of force techniques are NOT covered in ABST.**

It is highly recommended you take an appropriate course that focuses on:
- Use of force techniques
- Personal safety
- De-escalation strategies
- Physical intervention skills

### Remember

- Use force only when necessary
- Use the minimum force required
- Stop using force when the threat is neutralized
- Document all incidents thoroughly
- Report to police and your supervisor
- Never let your emotions dictate your actions

pages.title: Procedure for Making a Lawful Arrest
pages.body:
## Procedure for Making a Lawful Arrest

### Before You Decide to Arrest

**Best Practice: Wherever possible, do NOT arrest yourself.**

- Call the police and be a good witness
- Be observant and take clear, detailed notes
- Check your employer's policy—some prohibit staff from making arrests

### The Arrest Process: Step-by-Step

If you must make an arrest, follow these steps carefully:

#### Step 1: Identify Yourself

**Clearly state:**
- Your name
- Your role/position
- Your employer or the property you represent

**Example:**
> "My name is John. I am the loss prevention worker for Mega Mart."

**Why this matters:**
- The person needs to know you are not a random member of the public
- Establishes your authority
- Reduces confusion and potential resistance

#### Step 2: Tell the Person Why You Are Arresting Them

**State the specific offence:**
- Be clear and specific
- Name the offence
- Be brief

**Example:**
> "I am arresting you for theft of a video game."

**Why this matters:**
- Section 10 of the Charter requires people be informed promptly of reasons for arrest
- Prevents claims of unlawful detention
- Ensures the person understands the situation

#### Step 3: Check for Understanding

**Ask directly:**
- "Do you understand what I have just told you?"
- Wait for a response
- If they don't understand, repeat the information

**Why this matters:**
- Ensures clear communication
- Protects you legally
- Addresses language or comprehension barriers

#### Step 4: Remove the Person to a Private Area (If Possible)

**Politely direct them:**
- "Please come with me."
- Move to a secure, private location
- Examples: security office, back room, designated area

**Why this matters:**
- Protects the person's dignity
- Reduces public spectacle
- Provides safer environment for all involved
- Allows for better communication

**Safety Note:**
- Only remove to private area if safe to do so
- Consider having backup present
- Never isolate yourself with a potentially dangerous person

#### Step 5: Advise the Person of Their Rights (Optional)

**Important: There is NO legal requirement for a security professional to read rights or provide access to a lawyer.**

However, you may choose to:
- Allow them to make a phone call
- Advise they are not obligated to say anything

**Example statement:**
> "You are not obligated to say anything. If you say something, it may be recorded and used as evidence."

**Check with your supervisor or employer policy for guidance on this step.**

#### Step 6: Call the Police and Await Their Arrival

**This is MANDATORY under Section 494(3):**
- You must turn the arrested person over to a peace officer **forthwith** (immediately)
- Call police as soon as it is safe and practical to do so
- Remain with the arrested person until police arrive
- Do NOT transport them yourself
- Do NOT hand them to your supervisor instead of police

**When calling police, be prepared to provide:**
- Your location
- Description of the person arrested
- The offence committed
- Whether the person is cooperative or not
- Any safety concerns

### While Waiting for Police

**Do:**
- Remain calm and professional
- Keep the person in sight
- Take detailed notes of:
  - Time of arrest
  - What you witnessed
  - What the person said
  - Any witnesses present
- Preserve evidence (if applicable)
- Secure the scene

**Do NOT:**
- Interrogate the person
- Make promises ("You'll be out soon")
- Make threats
- Use force unless necessary for safety
- Allow the person to leave
- Discuss the case with bystanders

### When Police Arrive

**Be prepared to:**
- Provide a clear statement of what occurred
- Explain why you made the arrest
- Answer questions the officers have
- Provide your notes if requested
- Identify any witnesses
- Turn over any evidence

**The police will:**
- Take custody of the arrested person
- Decide whether to continue with charges
- Conduct their own investigation
- May ask you to provide a written statement later

### Record Keeping

**As soon as time permits, record in your notebook:**
- Date and time of incident
- Location (be specific)
- What you witnessed (the offence)
- Description of the person arrested
- What you said during the arrest
- What the person said or did
- Time police were called
- Time police arrived
- Names of officers who attended
- Names and contact information of witnesses
- Any evidence seized or observed
- Your actions throughout

### Common Mistakes to Avoid

1. **Not calling police immediately**
   - You MUST turn arrested person over forthwith

2. **Arresting when you didn't witness the offence**
   - You cannot arrest based on what someone tells you

3. **Using excessive force**
   - Use only the minimum force necessary

4. **Not identifying yourself**
   - Person must know who you are and why you have authority

5. **Detaining for too long without police**
   - Call police immediately

6. **Interrogating the arrested person**
   - Not your role—leave investigation to police

7. **Arresting for summary conviction offences under s. 494(1)(a)**
   - Only indictable and hybrid offences qualify

### Legal Protection

Following this procedure protects you from:
- Criminal charges (unlawful detention, assault)
- Civil lawsuits (false arrest, false imprisonment)
- Employment discipline or termination
- Damage to professional reputation

### Final Reminder

**Your primary duty is to observe and report, not to enforce the law.**

Arrest should be a last resort when:
- Police cannot attend in reasonable time
- Public safety requires immediate action
- Evidence will be lost if you don't act
- You have clearly witnessed an offence that qualifies under Section 494

pages.title: Search and Seizure, Trespassing, and Additional Legislation
pages.body:
## Search and Seizure, Trespassing, and Additional Legislation

### Search and Seizure

#### Fundamental Principle

**A security professional has NO power in law to search persons or their property.**

Section 8 of the Charter protects everyone from unreasonable search or seizure.

#### Search With Consent

The only way you may search someone or their property is with their **consent**.

**Common example:** Access control at large venues
- Concert halls
- Sporting events
- Convention centres
- Private functions

At these locations, you may require people to:
- Submit bags or backpacks for search
- Submit to personal search (pat-down)
- Before gaining entry to the facility

**How consent works:**
- The person retains the right to choose NOT to enter the building
- Therefore, they retain the right to NOT submit to the search
- In return, you have the right to deny them access if they refuse

**Important:** This is NOT mandatory search—it is conditional entry.

#### When Consent is Withdrawn

**If a person changes their mind during a search:**
- They may withdraw consent at any time
- You MUST stop searching immediately
- They then forfeit their right to enter the premises

#### Search After Arrest

**If you have arrested someone under Section 494, you may search them if:**
- You have reason to believe they are carrying a weapon
- They have evidence of the offence you witnessed
  - Example: Stolen merchandise in loss prevention situation
- They have items suggesting a means of escape
- They have stolen property on them

**Best Practice:** Do NOT search an individual yourself. Instead:
- Wait for police to arrive
- Advise officers of your concerns
- Request that police perform the search

### Trespassing Legislation

As a security professional, you will frequently encounter trespassers. Two main pieces of legislation apply:

#### Trespass to Premises Act

This Act provides clear authorization for you—as a representative of the property owner—to enforce its provisions.

**Section 2: What is Trespass?**

A person commits trespass when they enter or remain on premises after being given notice not to trespass.

**How notice can be given:**

**Method 1: Oral or Written Notice**
- Given by the owner OR
- Given by an authorized representative of the owner (you, as security)

**Method 2: Signs**
- Displayed at each entrance normally used
- In the case of outdoor premises:
  - At all fence corners, OR
  - If no fence, at each corner of the premises

**Deemed Notice:** When signs are properly displayed, people are deemed to have had notice not to trespass.

**Section 4: Vehicle Trespass**
- When trespass is committed by motor vehicle
- The driver is guilty of the offence

**Section 5: Arrest Without Warrant**

A trespasser may be arrested by:
- Any peace officer, OR
- The owner or authorized representative of the owner (you)

**If you arrest a trespasser:**
- You must deliver them to a peace officer as soon as practicable

#### Petty Trespass Act

You should also be familiar with offences defined in the Petty Trespass Act. (Note: The module text cuts off here, but this Act covers minor trespassing offences.)

### Responding to Trespassers

#### Step 1: Ask Them to Leave

**Best approach:**
- Identify yourself
- Politely but firmly ask them to leave
- If they comply, you have achieved your goal

#### Step 2: Call Police if They Refuse

**If the trespasser will not leave:**
- Call police to remove them
- Continue to observe
- Take detailed notes

#### Step 3: Consider Arrest (If Necessary)

**You have authority to arrest, but consider:**
- How long will police take to arrive?
- Is the person causing harm or just loitering?
- Is it safe for you to arrest them?
- What is your employer's policy?

**Decision framework:**

**If the person is simply loitering and appears harmless:**
- Continue to observe
- Record behaviour
- Wait for police

**If behaviour escalates to criminal activity (e.g., break and enter):**
- Reassess the situation
- Consider arrest if police response will take too long
- Prioritize safety—yours and others'

### Vandalism

Vandalism is different from simple trespassing because damage to property is occurring.

**Characteristics:**
- Act of violence to some degree
- Leaves behind destruction
- Costly to property owner
- Could create hazards

**Response approach:**

**Best action:**
- Call police
- Provide information
- Await their arrival
- Continue observing from safe distance

**You are within your rights to arrest, BUT:**
- Vandalism suggests potential for violence
- Make decisions with safety in mind
- Sometimes observation and comprehensive notes are the best course of action
- Your notes will help police catch those responsible

### Gaming and Liquor Act and Regulation

**Important: A security professional does NOT have authority under the Gaming and Liquor Act.**

However, you may work at locations where gaming or liquor are present:
- Sports stadiums
- Concert venues
- Convention centres
- Hotels
- Private functions

#### Required Training

**ProServe:**
- Required if your job involves liquor sales, service, or security in Alberta
- Mandatory as of January 1, 2010
- Administered by Alberta Gaming and Liquor Commission (AGLC)

**ProTect:**
- Training for security staff
- Improves observation and communication skills
- Prepares you to prevent, defuse, and manage customer disturbances
- Helps prevent violence escalation

**Note:** These are AGLC licensing requirements, not SSIA requirements. Bouncers do not require SSIA licensing, but there may be overlap in mixed-use facilities.

#### Example Scenario: Access Control

**Situation:** Guarding access to sports venue, directed to deny entrance to anyone with alcohol

**Your authority:**
- You CAN refuse entry
- You CANNOT seize the liquor

**Proper approach:**
- Communicate that the person has a choice:
  - Dispose of the liquor and enter, OR
  - Keep the liquor and not enter
- Make it clear they are making the choice
- Remain professional and respectful

### Employment Standards Code and Regulation

As a security professional, you should be familiar with the Employment Standards Code and Regulation as it applies to your work conditions, hours, pay, and employment rights.

(Note: Specific details were not provided in the module text.)

### Key Takeaways

**Search and Seizure:**
- No power to search without consent
- Consent can be withdrawn
- Search after arrest only in specific circumstances
- Best practice: let police search

**Trespassing:**
- Ask trespassers to leave first
- Call police if they refuse
- You have arrest authority but consider all factors
- Safety first—sometimes observation is best

**Vandalism:**
- More serious than trespassing due to damage
- Suggests potential for violence
- Prioritize safety when deciding whether to arrest

**Gaming and Liquor:**
- No authority under the Act
- May need ProServe/ProTect training
- Can refuse entry, cannot seize liquor
- Emphasize choice to the individual

pages.title: Observation Skills for Security Professionals
pages.body:
## Observation Skills for Security Professionals

Observation is one of the most important skills for a security professional. Your main goal is to protect persons and property. To know if they have been harmed, you must know what they look like normally. Only then can you assess whether damage or injury has occurred.

### What is Observation?

Observation is actually a series of three activities:

- **Noticing** – using your senses to pick up information
- **Interpreting** – understanding what the information means
- **Recalling** – remembering the information when you need it later

All three steps are critical. For example, if you notice something and interpret it correctly but cannot recall it when needed, your observation is incomplete.

---

## Noticing: Using Your Senses

When you notice things, you use at least two of your senses to process information. While vision is important, your other senses help you gather vital details.

### The Five Senses in Security Work

**Sight**
- Continuously scan the area while on patrol
- Commit details to memory
- Be watchful for change, or things that look different than before

**Sound**
- Become familiar with the "normal" sounds at your posting
- Practice being able to tell which direction sound is coming from
- Listen for breaking glass, alarms, unusual noises

**Smell**
- Notice smells such as fire, natural gas, gasoline, or perfume/cologne
- Unusual odours may indicate danger or unauthorized persons

**Touch**
- Feel changes in temperature; heat behind a wall or door may indicate fire, while a cold draft could indicate a door or window has been opened
- Check doors and windows to see if they are locked
- Take care when searching bags (watch for sharp objects)

**Taste**
- **Do not use your sense of taste to test unknown substances** – this is dangerous and not part of your duties

---

## Observing Persons

When observing people, pay attention to:

- **Physical description**: height, weight, build, age, gender
- **Facial features**: hair colour and style, eye colour, facial hair, distinctive marks (scars, tattoos)
- **Clothing**: type, colour, condition, logos or distinctive features
- **Behaviour**: body language, what they are doing, how they are moving
- **Speech**: accent, tone, unusual phrases
- **Direction of travel**: where they came from, where they are going

---

## Interpretation: Understanding What You Notice

Noticing things is vital, but you must also **apply the information** and respond accordingly.

### Making Sense of Information

When you see flames and smell smoke, you think about those things and determine there is fire. You then make decisions in response.

**Important considerations:**

- Sometimes your next action may be **no action** (e.g., everything appears normal during routine patrol)
- Sometimes information leads you to a clear conclusion (e.g., someone smashing a car window is clearly up to no good)
- **Avoid jumping to conclusions** when there could be more than one explanation
- When unsure, **gather additional information** by asking questions or observing more closely

### Example: The Anxious Employee

A man enters an office tower late in the evening, appearing anxious and in a hurry. He barely acknowledges you and rushes past your desk. You recognize him as an employee.

**Possible explanations:**
- He is coming in after hours to access something he shouldn't
- Someone is forcing him to do something he does not want to
- He forgot something important (like his wife's birthday gift) and is rushing to retrieve it

**Your response:** Gather more information by asking: *"Good evening sir, you seem to be in a bit of a hurry, is there something I can help you with tonight?"*

Based on his response, you will have a clearer understanding of the situation.

### Be Aware of Misleading Signs

Some situations can be misinterpreted:

- Individuals who have had **strokes or brain injuries** may have slurred speech and uncoordinated movement. They may appear intoxicated, but their physical impairment causes them to act this way.
- When unsure, **gather additional information** while remaining watchful and keeping your safety in mind.

---

## Recalling: Remembering Information

The final step in good observation is being able to **use the information later**. You need to recall details at a later point in time.

### The CAR Method for Better Recall

To improve your ability to remember information, use these three steps:

**C – Concentrate**
- Focus on a particular piece of information and block out everything else
- Example: If you hear an engine running, focus only on that sound. Notice changes in volume or pitch.

**A – Associate**
- Relate the detail to something you already know
- Example: If Rob says "I work at Mega Mart," you might remember better by thinking, "Rob works at the same place as my brother."

**R – Repeat**
- Repeat the information slowly and deliberately
- The more you practice, the easier it becomes
- Example: As you walk past stores in a mall, repeat the name of each manager in your mind

---

## Key Takeaways

✓ Observation involves noticing, interpreting, and recalling information

✓ Use all five senses (sight, sound, smell, touch – but never taste unknown substances)

✓ Avoid jumping to conclusions; gather additional information when needed

✓ Practice the CAR method (Concentrate, Associate, Repeat) to improve recall

✓ Document important observations in your notebook as soon as possible

✓ Good observation skills take practice – the more you use them, the better you become

pages.title: Patrol Procedures and Techniques
pages.body:
## Patrol Procedures and Techniques

Patrolling is making an active survey of the persons or property you are tasked with protecting. When you patrol, you make your presence known – in many cases, the sight of a uniformed guard is sufficient to keep potential troublemakers away.

However, patrolling is **more than just walking around**. It involves being actively aware of your surroundings and of changes to persons and property within those surroundings.

---

## Be Prepared Before Starting Your Shift

Before starting your shift, make sure you are physically and mentally prepared. You give yourself the best chance of being ready for an incident when you prepare before your shift begins.

### Physical Preparedness

**Health and Wellness**
- Eat right and exercise regularly
- Get enough sleep
- If you are sick, call your supervisor – going to work when ill is a hazard to yourself and others

**Uniform and Equipment**
- Ensure clothing is clean, pressed, and properly mended
- Make sure your uniform is complete (name badge, licence, etc.)
- Check that items on your duty belt are securely fastened, in good working order, and nothing is missing
- Have keys, notebook, pen, radio, or phone ready
- If you have a radio, test to ensure it is working

### Mental Preparedness

- Review **post orders** and discuss with supervisor if required
- Deal with personal distractions before your shift, or set a time to deal with them after
- **Get focused** on the task at hand
- Review your assignment and make a mental list of what you must accomplish

---

## Starting Your Patrol

### Survey the Area

Upon arriving at your post:

- Take time to **survey the area** – you need to know what things look like normally to determine when they have changed
- Familiarize yourself with any persons on site and anticipated arrivals
- Find out about any extra events (e.g., scheduled fire drill, site inspection)
- Knowing what to expect is part of being well-prepared

### Develop a Pattern

- **Develop a consistent pattern** for your patrol duties
- Once memorized, you will have one less thing to think about and can concentrate better on actual patrolling
- Example: Move from ground floor up through the top floor in a multi-storey building
- **Guard against complacency** – don't assume everything is fine just because it looks the same as last time

---

## Foot Patrol

Foot patrol is the most common method of patrolling and is appropriate for many settings: office buildings, retail settings, sport or concert venues, and airports.

### Advantages of Foot Patrol

- You are not distracted by operating a vehicle
- Easier to hear sounds (e.g., breaking glass) and notice smells (e.g., smoke)
- Allows you to use all your senses
- Makes it easier to stay close to persons or property you are protecting
- You can access secluded areas like stairwells
- More opportunity to interact with people and learn what is going on

### Important Points for Foot Patrol

**General Safety**
- If you can see into areas before entering (via CCTV), do so
- In parking lots or parkades, walk on the side of the roadway – you may not see or hear approaching cars
- **Do not use MP3 players or portable stereos** while on duty – they interfere with your hearing

**Smoking on Duty**
- Smoking affects your sense of smell
- In daylight, it detracts from your professional appearance
- At night, a lit cigarette announces your presence to potential intruders

**Night Patrol Considerations**
- Consider your stance with respect to light – avoid creating a silhouette of yourself
- Walk quietly so as not to announce your presence
- Startling a potential intruder may put you in an unsafe position

---

## Carrying Out a Basic Patrol

### Step-by-Step Process

**1. Bring Equipment**
- Carry your radio, notebook, pen, and any other standard equipment
- Be prepared in case you find trouble

**2. Walk Confidently**
- Be professional – others are watching you
- Exchange quick greetings if someone says hello
- Answer simple questions (e.g., where to park)
- **Do not become engrossed in lengthy conversations** – you are paid to protect persons and property, and conversations can be distracting

**3. Follow a Consistent Route**
- Ensure you cover all areas you are responsible for
- Be careful not to become so familiar that you stop looking closely

**4. Check Doors and Windows**
- If they should be locked, ensure they are with each pass

**5. Look for Signs Something is Amiss**
- Watch for people involved in odd behaviour (e.g., trying to open multiple car doors)
- Investigate objects or items that appear out of place
- Use your senses to detect when something is "not quite right"

**6. Use Lighting Properly**
- In poor lighting, shine your light into a room or space before entering
- This allows you to see what is there and prevents someone from hiding in the dark

**7. Record Observations**
- Document anything unusual in your notebook

**8. Contact Police When Necessary**
- Call police if you find evidence suggesting a criminal act has taken place

---

## Vehicle Patrol

Some employers provide a vehicle (car, SUV, bicycle) for patrols. This is more common for large sites like construction sites or large parking lots.

### Advantages of Vehicle Patrol

- Cover large areas more quickly than on foot
- Respond to events faster
- Carry more equipment (e.g., first aid kit)

### Disadvantages

- Distracted by operating the vehicle
- More difficult to hear sounds (breaking windows) or notice smells (smoke)
- Cannot access secluded areas easily
- Less opportunity to interact with people

---

## Understanding Your Role

When you receive orders from your employer:

- You will be given instructions about specific persons or events to watch for
- You may be provided with a list of individuals permitted to enter
- You may be asked to report certain events, even if they don't appear worrisome

### What is NOT Your Job

- **Do not monitor specific work activities** of persons permitted on site (e.g., length of breaks, quality of work)
- Your job is to be aware of their presence and observe that their activities are not harming the persons and property you protect
- If activity is causing harm, communicate about the damage – not about their skills or methods

---

## Key Takeaways

✓ Be physically and mentally prepared before starting your shift

✓ Survey the area and develop a consistent patrol pattern

✓ Guard against complacency – don't assume everything is fine

✓ Foot patrol allows you to use all your senses and interact with people

✓ Vehicle patrol is useful for large sites but has limitations

✓ Check doors and windows, look for signs of trouble, and document observations

✓ Contact police when you find evidence of criminal activity

pages.title: Access Control and Surveillance
pages.body:
## Access Control and Surveillance

As a security professional, you act on the owner's behalf to monitor and control access to persons or property as directed by your employer. You should receive clear instructions about who is permitted access and what is granted by allowing that access.

---

## Controlling Access

### Understanding Access Permissions

Depending on the setting, permissions may change from individual to individual.

**Examples:**
- **Concert venue**: People with floor tickets can only access floor seating; people with raised seating tickets cannot access the floor
- **Construction site**: Access to certain areas may only be given to those wearing appropriate safety equipment (hardhat, steel-toed boots)

### Important Principles

**It is NOT your job to question why access is different between persons**

- Do not let someone persuade you to allow access through a good argument or emotional plea
- If time and circumstances allow, note the request and advise the person you will bring it up with your supervisor
- If the individual is persistent and keeps you from your duties, call your supervisor or designated site contact

### Dealing with Persistent Individuals

If no supervisor or contact is available:

1. **Stand your ground** and continue to deny access
2. If they continue to be bothersome, **ask them to leave**
3. If they do not leave, **advise them you will treat the incident as trespassing**
4. If they still refuse to leave, **deal with them as you would any other trespasser**

---

## Access Near Critical Areas

Access to persons or property generally **decreases** near:

- **Individuals of status** (e.g., president of an organization)
- **Critical locations** within the property:
  - Mechanical systems rooms
  - Vaults
  - Areas near items of great value (prized art, expensive jewellery)

### Methods of Granting Access

Individuals permitted to enter controlled access areas are usually provided with:

- **Keys**
- **Swipe cards**
- **Tickets**
- **ID cards**
- **Invitations** (for certain functions)
- **Access lists** (document listing names of permitted individuals)
- **Recognition** (small settings where you are introduced to people who work or live there)

### Checking Identification

**Important Point:** Take care when looking at ID. **Make sure the photo on the card matches the individual standing before you.**

### Access Policies with Guests

In small settings where you recognize individuals by sight:

- Find out from your supervisor what the access policy is when those individuals bring another person with them

---

## Monitoring Departures

In some cases, you will be concerned with individuals who are **leaving** the premises.

**Common requirements:**
- Individuals must sign out when leaving
- Turn in equipment (e.g., keys) at the end of their shift

**Your responsibilities:**
- Find out what items must be collected
- Know what to do with items once they are in your possession

---

## Surveillance

Surveillance means carrying out patrol duties from a **fixed position**.

### Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV)

In many cases, you will have assistance from CCTV monitors.

**Settings where CCTV is commonly used:**
- Banks
- Office towers
- Retail stores
- Hotels

**Common Setups:**
- Security professional posted in a lobby watching monitors
- Monitors housed in a separate room with a guard keeping constant watch
- CCTV used along with mobile foot or vehicle patrols

### Surveillance Best Practices

**Use the same processes and safety considerations as foot patrol:**

- Remain observant and focused
- Document unusual activity
- Know when to call for assistance
- Follow post orders

---

## Video Footage from Labour Disputes

You may be required to gather video footage at a labour dispute.

### Why Video is Important

- Some picket lines erupt into violence and conflict
- Business owners suffer losses from vandalism
- Individuals may be injured from assault
- Video footage helps with investigations and court proceedings

### Handling Video Footage

**Important Rules:**

✓ **Secure footage immediately** and turn over to your supervisor as soon as possible

✓ Images taken without consent should be **used only for investigation purposes**

✗ **Do NOT release photos or video to the media**

✗ **Do NOT use footage on social networking sites or the Internet**

---

## Alarms

Another form of surveillance is monitoring alarms.

### Types of Alarms

**Fire Alarms**
- Most premises have fire alarms on site

**Security/Burglar Alarms**
- Increasing number of locations have these systems

**Building Operation Alarms**
- Monitor heating/cooling systems, water, etc.

### What You Need to Know

**When assuming your post:**

1. Find out what alarm system(s) are in place
2. Know what procedures to follow if an alarm sounds
3. Determine the location of the alarm panel
4. Understand any lights, sounds, or indicators that may be triggered
5. Familiarize yourself with basic operation if you need to operate the system

### Alarm Activation Procedures

**Automatic vs. Manual Notification**
- Some systems trigger automatic requests for help
- Others require manual notification to emergency services

**Your Duties During an Alarm:**
- Call in the alarm (if manual system)
- Perform a check for occupants
- Ensure individuals leave premises in a safe, orderly manner
- **Remember**: You are hired to protect designated persons or property – this remains true during an alarm

**Investigating Alarms:**
- You may be instructed to investigate the cause of an alarm
- A dispatcher may call to advise an alarm has been received and ask you to follow up

---

## Alarm Responders

Many organizations and homeowners contract security firms to handle after-hours alarm responses.

### Responding to an Alarm Call

When dispatched to respond to an alarm:

**1. Travel Safely**
- **Obey all traffic laws** when traveling to the location
- As a security professional, you have **no special authority** to speed or ignore traffic signals

**2. Upon Arrival**
- Check if doors and windows are secure
- Note any that appear open, unlocked, or otherwise unsecured

**3. If Evidence of Break-In or Criminal Activity**
- **Notify police immediately**
- Document your findings
- **Take care not to displace or destroy evidence**

**4. Remain Observant**
- Be continually observant while at the premises

**5. Unsecured Premises**
- If you find premises not secure (open/unlocked door or window), notify police
- Police will advise if they will attend the scene

**6. Contact Key-Holder**
- Follow protocol for advising the alarm key-holder (usually a company employee) to reset the system

**7. Documentation**
- Document events in your notebook
- Prepare any reports as required

---

## Key Takeaways

✓ Act on the owner's behalf to control access as directed by your employer

✓ Do not question why access differs between persons – follow your instructions

✓ Always check photo ID matches the individual before you

✓ Stand your ground if someone tries to persuade you to grant unauthorized access

✓ CCTV surveillance requires the same care and attention as foot patrol

✓ Video footage from labour disputes must be secured and never released to media or posted online

✓ Familiarize yourself with alarm systems and procedures at your posting

✓ When responding to alarms, obey all traffic laws and notify police of any criminal activity

pages.title: Crowd Control Techniques
pages.body:
## Crowd Control Techniques
